_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the IA Corn Belt cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across two eras (1860-1920 horse era, 1920-1960 mechanized era). Corn was the defining crop — the corn-hog cycle dictated the calendar. Unlike the dairy counties of the ia_eastern cluster, the Corn Belt calendar had a true slack season (December-March) and intense peak labor (May planting, August-October harvest).
display_name: IA Corn Belt — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Corn production, hog feeding, small grains (oats), hay, dairy (supplementary)

calendar_eras.description: The corn-hog calendar was defined by the growing season. Unlike the dairy farm calendar (year-round milking), the corn farm had distinct seasons: intense spring planting, summer cultivation, fall harvest, and a true winter slack. The corn-hog cycle layered livestock management on top of the crop calendar — hogs were farrowed in spring, fattened on corn through fall, and shipped in November. The oat crop and the hay crop filled the gaps. This calendar demanded peak labor in May (planting) and September-October (harvest) and allowed relative rest in January-February. Farm families used the slack season for community life — church, school, visiting, and the courthouse business that the growing season pushed aside.

january.agricultural: Winter slack. Livestock feeding — hogs fed corn, cattle fed hay. Equipment repair. Woodlot work for fuel and fence posts. Manure hauled when ground was frozen. No field work possible.
january.social_civic: The most social month. Church activities — Ladies Aid, men's club, card parties. School in session (rural schools October-April). Hog butchering — a communal event. Farm Institute meetings (agricultural education). Wedding season in the slack period.
january.economic: Year-end accounts. Tax collection. Land transactions peaked in winter when farmers had time. Seed orders placed. Implement dealers promoted spring sales.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — average January temperature 18-22F across the cluster. Minimums of -20 to -30F recorded regularly. Snow cover typical. The flat, treeless prairie offered no wind protection — blizzards could be lethal.
january.narrative_hook: January {year} in {county} County was the rest the corn calendar allowed — {ancestor_name} fed the livestock, repaired the equipment, and attended to the community business that the growing season had pushed aside since April.

february.agricultural: Same as January — livestock feeding, equipment repair. Seed cleaning. Early planning for spring. Some manure hauling if ground was frozen hard.
february.social_civic: Churches continued regular programs. Farm Bureau and cooperative meetings. Rural school in session. Political caucuses and township meetings.
february.economic: Monthly cream check (for farms with dairy cattle). Tax settlement. Implement dealers displayed spring equipment. Seed corn purchased from local dealers or Iowa State-connected sources.
february.weather_conditions: Often the coldest month — average 20-24F. February blizzards could be severe. Daylight lengthening. Late February occasionally brought premature thaw.
february.narrative_hook: February {year} was the month when {ancestor_name} counted the hay in the mow and the corn in the crib — the livestock had to eat until April, and the arithmetic of winter was whether the feed supply would outlast the cold.

march.agricultural: Spring preparation. Oat ground plowed if early thaw permitted. Pasture fences inspected. Hog lots prepared for spring farrowing. Equipment readied for planting. Manure spread on fields.
march.social_civic: Easter preparation (if March). School spring term. Cooperative annual meetings. County agent spring meetings on planting plans.
march.economic: Spring credit arranged at the bank and feed store. Seed purchased. Cooperative elevator cleaned out remaining old-crop grain.
march.weather_conditions: Highly variable — freeze-thaw cycles, late snow, early warm spells. Average 32-40F. Mud season — the spring breakup that made roads impassable for 2-4 weeks. The poorly drained Des Moines Lobe soils were the worst.
march.narrative_hook: March {year} was the month of mud — the frost leaving {county} County's black soil in waves, the section-line road dissolving into a track that swallowed the wagon wheels, and the fields too wet to touch until the drainage tile did its work.

april.agricultural: Oats seeded — the first crop in the ground. Spring plowing for corn ground. Hog farrowing began — spring pigs were the year's income in miniature. Pastures greened. Cattle turned out. Garden planting.
april.social_civic: Easter services. Minimal organized social activity — spring work consumed every hour. School winding down.
april.economic: Peak credit extension. Seed and supplies purchased. Oats in the ground — the first investment of the growing season.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — average 48-54F. Spring rains and thunderstorms. Tornado season began. Flooding along the Iowa River and Raccoon River drainages. The tile systems running full, carrying winter's moisture to the ditches.
april.narrative_hook: April {year} was the race — {ancestor_name} had to get the oats in the ground before the soil dried too much, start the hog farrowing, fix the fences, and watch the tile outlets running brown water that said the field was draining and the corn ground was getting ready.

may.agricultural: CORN PLANTING — the critical event of the year. Corn planted after soil reached 50F, typically mid-May. Horse-drawn planter placed seeds in check-rows (hills spaced 40 inches apart in both directions, allowing cross-cultivation). Hay fields growing for first cutting. Hog farrowing continued.
may.social_civic: Decoration Day (May 30) — cemetery observance. Church confirmation. School ended for rural schools. Iowa State College commencement at Ames.
may.economic: Corn planting represented the year's largest investment in seed and labor. No income yet — all outgo.
may.weather_conditions: Warm — average 60-66F. Last frost typically mid-May in the cluster. Thunderstorms frequent. Adequate moisture critical for corn germination. A wet May delayed planting and reduced yields — 'plant corn by May 20 or take a 10% loss' was the rule.
may.narrative_hook: May {year} was corn planting — {ancestor_name} followed the team and the check-row planter across the black field, dropping kernels in a grid so precise that the rows ran straight in both directions, because straight rows meant the cultivator could run both ways and the weeds could not hide.

june.agricultural: Corn cultivation — the horse-drawn cultivator ran between the rows (and across the rows in check-planted fields) for weed control. First hay cutting — timothy, clover, alfalfa. Oats maturing. Garden in full production. Hog litters growing.
june.social_civic: County fairs began. Church picnics and suppers. School out — children in the fields.
june.economic: First hay income for farms that sold hay. Cream checks for dairy-supplemented farms. No corn income yet.
june.weather_conditions: Summer — average 70-76F. Humid. Thunderstorms frequent. Hail risk — a June hailstorm could destroy a corn crop before it reached knee height. The flat terrain offered no protection.
june.narrative_hook: June {year} was cultivating time — {ancestor_name} ran the cultivator through the corn rows four times, stirring the black soil between the plants, killing the weeds that would steal the moisture and the sunlight that the corn needed to make the year's income.

july.agricultural: Second cultivation of corn. Hay second cutting. Corn in tassel by late July — the pollination window that determined the ear fill and the year's yield. Oats maturing for August harvest. Garden at peak. Canning began.
july.social_civic: Independence Day (July 4) — community celebrations, baseball, picnics, political speeches. County fairs. Church ice cream socials.
july.economic: Midseason assessment. The corn crop was visible — knee-high by the Fourth was the benchmark. Oat harvest approaching.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — average 76-82F. Humidity high. Thunderstorms daily in some weeks. Hail and tornado risk at peak. Drought stress visible in corn if July rain was insufficient — curling leaves, poor pollination.
july.narrative_hook: July {year} in {county} County was the anxious month — the corn tasseling in the heat, the thunderheads building every afternoon, and {ancestor_name} watching the sky and knowing that the difference between a good year and a ruined one was whether the storm brought rain or hail.

august.agricultural: OAT HARVEST — cut with a binder, shocked, threshed by steam-powered separator. Threshing crew of 12-20 men moved from farm to farm — the largest cooperative labor event of the year. Third hay cutting. Corn entering grain-fill stage. Canning intensive — corn, beans, tomatoes.
august.social_civic: Threshing was the social event of harvest — the crew ate three enormous meals per day prepared by the farmwife and neighborhood women. County fairs. Church events.
august.economic: Oat income — the first grain cash of the year. Threshing costs paid to the threshing ring operator. The threshing dinner was the farmwife's contribution to the cooperative labor system.
august.weather_conditions: Warm and humid — average 74-78F. Rain during threshing was a disaster — wet oats spoiled in the shock. The prayer for dry weather during threshing was universal.
august.narrative_hook: When the threshing crew reached {ancestor_name}'s farm in August {year}, the kitchen became a factory — {pronoun_possessive} wife fed twenty men three meals a day while the separator roared in the oat field and the year's grain income poured into the wagon box.

september.agricultural: Corn maturing. Silo filling — corn cut green and chopped for silage (on farms with dairy cattle). Fall plowing began. Hay third cutting. Hogs fattening on corn. Apple harvest from farm orchards.
september.social_civic: School resumed. County fairs — livestock shows, produce competitions. Church fall suppers and bazaars. Political organizing for November elections.
september.economic: Early corn could be sold. Hog prices watched closely — November shipping dependent on fall prices. Cream checks for dairy farms.
september.weather_conditions: Cooling — average 64-68F. First frost late September to mid-October. Beautiful weather — dry, clear, cool mornings.
september.narrative_hook: September {year} was the beautiful pause — the corn standing golden in the fields, the oats already threshed, and {ancestor_name} waiting for the ears to dry so the picking could begin, in a county where the sky was the color of autumn and the air smelled like the end of the growing season.

october.agricultural: CORN PICKING — the most labor-intensive harvest task. Ears stripped by hand from dried stalks and thrown into a wagon. A good picker did 80-100 bushels per day. The bang board on the far side of the wagon was the target. Fall plowing continued. Root cellar filled. Hog butchering began.
october.social_civic: October was the most social month of fall. Church bazaars, community suppers. Political rallies. The last pleasant outdoor events before winter.
october.economic: Corn income — the year's largest single crop revenue. Hog shipping to Chicago — corn-fed hogs at market weight. Land transactions at the courthouse.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — average 52-58F. First hard frost (below 28F) mid-to-late October. The flat landscape and enormous sky made October sunsets spectacular. Harvest dust in the air.
october.narrative_hook: October {year} in {county} County was the sound of corn hitting the bang board — {ancestor_name} stripped ears from the stalks with gloved hands, threw them against the wagon side, and counted the year's income in loads hauled to the elevator.

november.agricultural: Corn picking completed. Fall plowing finished before freeze-up. Hog shipping — the annual cash event. Livestock moved to winter quarters. Wood cutting for fuel.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Election Day. Church Thanksgiving services. Hunting season — pheasant and quail on the stubble fields.
november.economic: Year-end settlement. Hog checks deposited. Accounts balanced at the bank and the store. Credit for winter provisions.
november.weather_conditions: Cold setting in — average 36-40F. First snow. Freeze-up. November storms could be severe on the flat prairie.
november.narrative_hook: November {year} was the closing — {ancestor_name} finished the corn, shipped the hogs, settled the accounts, and stacked the wood for winter. The corn crib was full or it wasn't, and the number told the story of the year.

december.agricultural: Winter routine — livestock feeding, equipment maintenance. No field work. The corn calendar's true rest.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount celebration. Church programs, children's concerts. Wedding season. Community suppers and card parties. The slack season's social peak.
december.economic: Year-end. Christmas shopping at the county-seat stores and through catalogs. Credit accounts settled or carried.
december.weather_conditions: Winter — average 22-26F. Snow cover. Shortest days. The flat prairie wind was the constant — nothing between {ancestor_name}'s farmstead and the Arctic but a wire fence.
december.narrative_hook: December {year} in {county} County was the rest the corn calendar gave — {ancestor_name} fed the livestock, repaired the harness, and attended the Christmas program at the church, knowing that the next planting season was four months away and the corn crib's contents were the measure of the year that had passed.

slack_season: December-February (livestock feeding only — true slack unlike dairy)

record_generation_calendar.description: The corn-hog calendar produced predictable record patterns. Hog shipping in November generated sales receipts. Corn elevator receipts in October-November. Court sessions scheduled around the agricultural calendar. Marriage licenses peaked in December-February (slack season). Land transactions peaked in February-March (pre-planting planning) and October-November (post-harvest capital available).

county_variations.variation: Iowa State College academic calendar overlaid — commencement in May, enrollment in September. Extension Service programming year-round.
Coal mining calendar was year-round — mines operated regardless of season. Mining families had no agricultural slack season.
Railroad and factory calendar overlaid — shift work at Fisher Controls and railroad shops was independent of the farm calendar. Town and country ran on different clocks.
Meskwaki Settlement followed its own calendar — the annual powwow (August) was the major cultural event. Meskwaki agricultural practices combined corn farming with traditional seasonal rhythms.
Storm Lake ice harvest in January-February was a winter industry. Lake recreation extended the social calendar into summer.
Drainage district maintenance was a spring responsibility — cleaning ditches before the tile systems needed to function.

narrative_hooks: The corn calendar gave {ancestor_name} what the dairy calendar did not — a winter rest, three months when the field work stopped and the only obligations were the livestock and the community that filled the slack season with church, school, and visiting.

In {county} County, the year was measured in corn — planted in May, cultivated in June, tasseled in July, picked in October — and everything else in {ancestor_name}'s life fit into the gaps between the corn's demands.
The threshing crew was the Corn Belt's cooperative labor — twenty men who moved from farm to farm, threshing oats at each one, fed by the farmwife, and paying their debt in labor rather than money.

October in {county} County was the sound of corn hitting the bang board — {ancestor_name} picking by hand, ears flung against the wagon side, seventy or eighty bushels a day, the year's income measured in loads hauled to the elevator.

The slack season — December through February — was when {ancestor_name} did everything the corn had postponed: fixed the roof, attended to the courthouse, courted the neighbor's daughter, and planned the next year's planting.
The hog shipping in November was the annual payday — corn-fattened hogs loaded onto the railroad, shipped to Chicago, and the check that came back was the Corn Belt's annual harvest of cash.
source_references: Schwieder, Dorothy. Iowa: The Middle Land. Ames: Iowa State University Press, 1996.
Ross, Earle D. Iowa Agriculture: An Historical Survey. Iowa City: State Historical Society of Iowa, 1951.
Federal Writers' Project. Iowa: A Guide to the Hawkeye State. New York: Viking Press, 1938.
Prior, Jean C. Landforms of Iowa. Iowa City: University of Iowa Press, 1991.
Iowa Department of Agriculture. Annual Reports, 1890-1960.
